01
The Book

We live in an age obsessed with looking modern — the right phone, the right slang, the right performance of confidence.

But what if most of what we call "modern" is actually its opposite?

Satire Social Critique Gen-Z Identity Enlightenment → Algorithms
Tap the blacked-out lines below to declassify them ↓

This book draws a hard line between true progress and mere noise — status, show-off, and shallow rebellion mistaken for growth.

Tracing modernity from the Enlightenment to our algorithm-driven present, its theme — The Lie of Being Modern — is a direct address to young readers caught between discipline and dopamine.

It is an uncomfortable, unflinching call to rebuild identity from the inside out, before mistaking the noise for the music.

From the author

"I hope this book doesn't just change your life —"

"— but quietly shapes the society you build." That's the one-line thesis stamped across the back cover, and it's the closest thing this book has to a mission statement.

🛍️

Consumption ≠ Identity

The shopping bags on the cover aren't decoration — they're the argument. Buying the look isn't building the self.

🗯️

Outrage ≠ Opinion

The screaming stick figures are two sides of the same performance: loud, reactive, and mistaking volume for conviction.

🚬

Vice & Defiance, Not Despair

A cigarette and a middle finger spelling out "IT" — not nihilism, but an optimistic dare to question modernity instead of just performing it.

"An uncomfortable, unflinching call to rebuild identity from the inside out — before mistaking the noise for the music."

— from the back cover
02
About the Author

Nitinjeet Sahu

Nitinjeet Sahu is a young Indian author who writes from raw fact rather than polished theory. According to his publisher listing, he began his literary journey at a very early age and is counted among the youngest authors in India to publish a motivational book.[1]

His debut, The Last Exam: Exploring the Inner Soul of a UPSC Aspirant (also listed as ...UPSC Warrior), was published on Amazon on July 27, 2025, running 258 pages. Retailer listings describe it as a reflection on the discipline, burnout, and emotional weight carried by aspirants preparing for India's civil services examinations.[2][3]

How to F*ck Modernity and Get Away With It — Modernity Misunderstood is his latest work, presented on its own back cover as a satirical, unflinching look at the gap between looking modern and actually progressing.

Sources
  • [1] Pothi.com — store.pothi.com, author bio & listing for "The Last Exam"
  • [2] Amazon.com — product listing, publication details
  • [3] Amazon.in — product listing, India edition
